title: UnCLe: Towards Scalable Dynamic Causal Discovery in Non-linear Temporal Systems
abstract: Uncovering cause-effect relationships from observational time series is fundamental to understanding complex systems. While many methods infer static causal graphs, real-world systems often exhibit dynamic causality-where relationships evolve over time. Accurately capturing these temporal dynamics requires time-resolved causal graphs. We propose UnCLe, a novel deep learning method for scalable dynamic causal discovery. UnCLe employs a pair of Uncoupler and Recoupler networks to disentangle input time series into semantic representations and learns inter-variable dependencies via auto-regressive Dependency Matrices. It estimates dynamic causal influences by analyzing datapoint-wise prediction errors induced by temporal perturbations. Extensive experiments demonstrate that UnCLe not only outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on static causal discovery benchmarks but, more importantly, exhibits a unique capability to accurately capture and represent evolving temporal causality in both synthetic and real-world dynamic systems (e.g., human motion). UnCLe offers a promising approach for revealing the underlying, time-varying mechanisms of complex phenomena.

paper_title: UnCLe: Towards Scalable Dynamic Causal Discovery in Non-linear Temporal Systems

objective.answer: The primary objective of the paper is to propose UnCLe, a novel deep learning method for scalable dynamic causal discovery in non-linear temporal systems. The authors aim to accurately capture and represent evolving temporal causality in both synthetic and real-world dynamic systems.
objective.evidence: We propose UnCLe, a novel deep learning method for scalable dynamic causal discovery. UnCLe employs a pair of Uncoupler and Recoupler networks to disentangle input time series into semantic representations and learns inter-variable dependencies via auto-regressive Dependency Matrices.

knowledge_gap.answer: The paper addresses the gap in accurately capturing dynamic causal structures through time-resolved causal graphs, which is essential for understanding complex systems where causal influences evolve over time.
knowledge_gap.evidence: The predominant paradigm in temporal causal discovery has largely focused on inferring static causal graphs, which represent an aggregated or time-averaged view of dependencies, thereby obscuring the rich, evolving nature of causality in many real-world phenomena.

answer: UnCLe introduces a novel deep learning framework specifically engineered for scalable discovery and representation of dynamic causal graphs.
The method combines semantic disentanglement of time series with perturbation-based, datapoint-wise error analysis to identify and quantify dynamic causal influences.
UnCLe demonstrates superior ability to uncover and track evolving causal structures through extensive experiments on synthetic datasets and real-world systems.
evidence: UnCLe’s core innovation lies in its ability to first disentangle complex, multivariate time series into meaningful semantic channels using a pair of parameter-sharing Uncoupler and Recoupler networks.
UnCLe infers time-resolved causal influences by meticulously analyzing datapoint-wise prediction errors that are induced by targeted temporal perturbations of individual series.
We demonstrate UnCLe’s superior ability to uncover and track evolving causal structures through extensive experiments on synthetic datasets with known dynamic ground truths.

inspirational_papers.answer:
- Granger (1969) Investigating causal relations by econometric models and cross-spectral methods. (Methodological precursors)
- Runge et al. (2019) Detecting and quantifying causal associations in large nonlinear time series datasets. (Experimental baselines)
- Tank et al. (2021) Neural Granger causality. (Papers with limitations addressed by this work)
inspirational_papers.evidence: While foundational approaches to temporal causal discovery, such as Granger causality [7] and its various linear (e.g., VAR-based) and nonlinear extensions (e.g., constraint-based methods like PCMCI [22], or early neural network adaptations [27, 17]), have laid crucial groundwork for inferring causal links from time series data.

steps.input: Input multivariate time series dataset x ∈RN×T
steps.output: Trained UnCLe model with learned semantic representations and Dependency Matrices
steps.evidence: Given an input multivariate time series dataset x ∈RN×T, UnCLe first trains its core architecture. This architecture consists of a pair of parameter-sharing Uncoupler and Recoupler networks, along with a set of auto-regressive Dependency Matrices (Ψ).

steps.input: Trained UnCLe model and input dataset
steps.output: Dynamic causal graph ˆGPert and static causal graph ˆGAgg
steps.evidence: Subsequent to training, UnCLe infers causal relationships. For dynamic causal discovery, individual time series xi within the input dataset are chronologically perturbed to disrupt their temporal structure.

tools.name: Uncoupler and Recoupler Networks
tools.description: Used for disentangling input time series into semantic representations
tools.evidence: The Uncoupler and Recoupler form the backbone of UnCLe’s representation learning, functioning akin to a parameter-sharing Temporal Convolutional Network (TCN) autoencoder.

tools.name: Auto-regressive Dependency Matrices
tools.evidence: To model inter-variable dependencies, UnCLe introduces a set of C lightweight Dependency Matrices, Ψ = {Ψ1, . . . , ΨC}.

benchmark_datasets.name: Lorenz96
benchmark_datasets.data_description: ODE model to simulate climate dynamics
benchmark_datasets.usage: Used for evaluating static causal discovery performance
benchmark_datasets.evidence: Lorenz96 [14] is a ODE model to simulate climate dynamics used by [16, 27, 10, 29, 5].

benchmark_datasets.name: MoCap
benchmark_datasets.data_description: Sensor observations reflecting biomechanics of human body
benchmark_datasets.usage: Used for evaluating dynamic causal discovery performance
benchmark_datasets.evidence: The Motion Capture (MoCap) dataset contains sensor observations reflecting the sophisticated biomechanics of the human body.

evaluation_metrics.name: AUROC
evaluation_metrics.purpose: Measures the area under the receiver operating characteristic curve
evaluation_metrics.application: Used to evaluate causal discovery accuracy on static graphs
evaluation_metrics.evidence: On all Lorenz datasets, UnCLe(P) perturbation consistently achieves the highest AUROC and AUPRC scores.

evaluation_metrics.name: AUPRC
evaluation_metrics.purpose: Measures the area under the precision-recall curve
evaluation_metrics.application: Used to evaluate causal discovery accuracy on static graphs
evaluation_metrics.evidence: On all Lorenz datasets, UnCLe(P) perturbation consistently achieves the highest AUROC and AUPRC scores.

baselines.name: VAR
baselines.description: Classic Granger causality method based on pairwise VAR F-tests.
baselines.evidence: We compare UnCLe against nine baseline methods spanning a range of categories, including constraint-based, score-based, and cutting-edge neural Granger approaches: (i) VAR, the classic Granger causality method [7] based on pairwise VAR F-tests.

baselines.name: PCMCI
baselines.description: Constraint-based approach that uses partial correlation for independence tests.
baselines.evidence: We compare UnCLe against nine baseline methods spanning a range of categories, including constraint-based, score-based, and cutting-edge neural Granger approaches: (ii) PCMCI, a constraint-based approach [22] that uses partial correlation for independence tests.

limitations.name: Lack of Formal Identifiability Guarantees
limitations.description: The paper does not provide a theoretical proof under which conditions UnCLe is guaranteed to recover the true dynamic causal graph.
limitations.evidence: The primary limitation of our work, which also defines a critical direction for future research, is the lack of formal identifiability guarantees.

future_directions.name: Establish Theoretical Conditions
future_directions.description: Focus on bridging the gap by exploring connections to causal representation learning and imposing further structural constraints on the model.
future_directions.evidence: Our future work will focus on bridging this gap, potentially by exploring connections to causal representation learning and imposing further structural constraints on the model.
